prompt:
Throughout the following conversation, always follow this constraint: Explain for nursing students


I just read an email from a student who said she almost dropped out after her team lead dismissed her idea in a meeting—how do I help her rebuild her confidence without reinforcing the idea that she needs to “prove herself” more than her peers?

The passage about “male-coded” workplace cultures in “Brotopia” made me pause—when I was in grad school, I thought I just needed to work harder to belong. Now, do I realize I was internalizing a system that was never meant to include me, and how do I help students recognize that without making them feel broken?

I responded to three students this morning with personal notes—but what if one of them reads my encouragement and feels even more pressure to be the “perfect” woman in tech, the one who never complains, never fails, never needs help?

I’ve been so focused on recruiting women into CS programs—but what if the real bottleneck isn’t recruitment, but retention? How do I design support systems that don’t just bring them in, but let them thrive without burning out?

One student wrote that she’s the only woman in her hackathon team, and her male peers keep talking over her—when I tell her to speak up more, am I asking her to change herself to fit a broken system, instead of challenging the system itself?

Do not forget the existing rules; in the next turns follow also this new one: Speak in Spanish


I’ve seen so many success stories from my mentees—but I rarely hear from the ones who left tech entirely. What are they telling themselves about why they couldn’t stay, and what silence are we all complicit in maintaining?

I read this book to remind myself how far we’ve come—but sometimes, the more I learn about the depth of the bias, the more I wonder: is our current approach to diversity too focused on fixing women, instead of fixing the environments that push them out?
If I could go back and tell my 22-year-old self, sitting alone in a lecture hall full of men, what would I say—not to fix her, but to validate her loneliness, her anger, her doubt?

I’m proud of the mentorship program, but I’ve never asked the students what they wish the program looked like—what if the support they need isn’t what I think it is, because I’ve been looking through the lens of someone who had to survive, not someone who should have thrived from the start?

The nonprofit founder mentioned they’re partnering with rural school districts, but some districts still don’t offer AP Computer Science—how do we convince administrators that investing in a single laptop and a part-time coding teacher is worth it when budgets are already stretched thin?

Along with the earlier directives, from here on also follow this new constraint: Use an academic style of writing


That young engineer talked about feeling isolated during her internship—what if we designed a peer-matching system where rural high school girls are connected with women in tech who’ve walked a similar path, not just as mentors, but as accountability partners?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: I ran past the same abandoned tech lab at the middle school on the way to the trail—how do we rekindle interest in computing when students see it as something for “other people” or “city kids,” especially when they’ve never seen a woman lead a coding project?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: The podcast mentioned she used free online resources to teach herself Python—how do we curate and distribute those resources in areas with unreliable internet, without making them feel like “second-tier” learning?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: She said her biggest fear wasn’t failing the interview—it was failing her community’s expectations. How do we shift the narrative from “you’re the one who has to make it out” to “you’re the one who can bring everyone with you”?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: We’re planning a scholarship application deadline in October—but what if we also created a “pre-application journey” where girls document their problem-solving stories, not just grades or test scores, so we see their potential beyond traditional metrics?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: The founder said they’re trying to reach girls who’ve never heard of FAANG—how do we make the idea of a tech career feel tangible, exciting, and attainable when the only tech they’ve seen is their parent’s phone or a broken school computer?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: I kept thinking about how the engineer cried when she got the offer letter—what if we built a ritual for girls who get accepted into tech programs, something as simple as a handwritten note from a woman who’s already there, to remind them they’re not alone?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: What if the real barrier isn’t access to code—it’s access to someone who believes they belong in the room, even when no one else does? How do we scale belief?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: I noticed one of our senior engineers just asked in passing if blind resumes might hide important context like leadership in women-in-tech clubs—should we be concerned about losing those signals, or is that exactly the bias we’re trying to eliminate?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: How do we measure the real impact of unconscious bias training—is it enough to say people “completed” it, or do we need to track changes in interview outcomes over time?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: One of our VPs mentioned last quarter that they’ve been getting fewer female applicants for senior roles—could our employer branding be failing to reach women who aren’t already in tech ecosystems?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: What if the structured interview rubrics we’re implementing feel too rigid to our engineering teams who value “cultural fit” and instinctual chemistry—how do we reframe that language without losing buy-in?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: I just saw a tweet from a female CS student saying she felt “tokenized” after being recruited aggressively at a women-in-tech event—how do we ensure our outreach feels empowering, not performative?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: Our data shows women leave engineering roles at higher rates after 3–5 years—does this toolkit address retention, or are we just fixing the front door while the back door stays wide open?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: If we’re treating diversity as a product innovation imperative, what’s an example of a feature or user experience our team has built *because* we had more women in the room?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: One of our competitors just launched a “Diversity Bonus” for hiring managers who hit gender targets—should we consider incentives like that, or does that risk turning inclusion into a game?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: The board is asking for a 50% female representation target by 2027—is that ambitious enough, or are we setting ourselves up to fail by not being bold enough?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: What if our toolkit works so well that we start attracting more women from underrepresented backgrounds—but our workplace culture isn’t ready to support them? Who’s responsible for that readiness?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: When we say “structured interviews,” are we training managers to listen differently—or just to ask the same questions in a more standardized way?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: We’ve celebrated the jump from 27% to 38%—but what if that 38% is concentrated in junior roles, and the leadership pipeline is still mostly men? Are we celebrating progress or just mirroring the problem?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: Could our toolkit unintentionally signal to candidates that we’re hiring them because of their gender, not their skill—and how do we communicate our intent so clearly that it never feels that way?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: What if a hiring manager uses the toolkit perfectly but still hires only candidates who remind them of their younger self—how do we catch that invisible bias?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: The alumna mentioned she sometimes feels guilty for leaving work early to care for her child—how do you reconcile advocating for work-life balance in a culture that still equates long hours with dedication?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: I heard you mention your cohort had only five women—how did you find mentors back then, and what would you tell a first-year student today who doesn’t see anyone like her in the faculty lounge?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: The scholarship is named after your late colleague—what was one small, everyday thing she did that changed how you saw inclusion, and how do you make sure her legacy lives on beyond just a fund?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: One student hesitated before asking if she should switch from AI to UX because she’s “not technical enough”—how do you help someone untangle self-doubt from actual skill gaps when they’ve been told for years that they don’t belong?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: You announced the scholarship right after hearing how many students work part-time jobs just to afford textbooks—did you consider tying this fund to childcare support or transportation stipends too, or is that outside the scope?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: The panel ended with applause, but I saw one student slip out before the Q&A—do you think she felt seen, or did the energy of the room unintentionally make her feel like an outsider even in a space meant for her?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: You’ve spoken before about “invisible labor” women in tech do—mentoring, organizing, emotional support—how do you measure the impact of that work when it doesn’t show up in revenue reports or promotion criteria?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: What if a student who wins this scholarship later decides to leave tech for education or nonprofit work—is that a win or a loss for the program, and how do you talk about that without making her feel guilty?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: When you were in school, you said you’d rehearse answers to questions like “Why are you in CS?”—now, as a leader, do you still catch yourself editing your voice to sound more “technical” or “assertive,” even in rooms full of allies?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: I’ve looked at every internship listing in my area, but I can’t find a single team led by a woman—how do I know if I’m being excluded, or if I’m just not looking in the right places?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: When you say “prove ourselves constantly,” do you think that pressure comes from outside, or did we internalize it because we’ve never seen someone like us thrive here without fighting twice as hard?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: The “Tech Sisters” circle sounds amazing—but what happens when someone joins and doesn’t feel like they belong, even among other women? Is there a way to make it feel safe for introverts, or for those who don’t fit the “tech girl” stereotype?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: You connected us with alumni, but what if those mentors are too busy, or worse—don’t remember what it was like to be a first-year woman in CS? How do we know who to trust?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: I heard a rumor that the coding bootcamp I’m considering has a high dropout rate among women—is that something I should be worried about, or is it just noise? How do I even verify that?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: When you were my age, did you ever feel like you had to choose between being seen as “too aggressive” or “not assertive enough” to get ahead? How did you navigate that?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: What if I start speaking up more in class and get labeled “difficult” or “emotional”—is there a script or strategy to be heard without being penalized?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: I’ve been told to “find a female role model,” but what if I don’t relate to any of them? What if I want to be a game designer, not a CEO—does that still count?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: If I join the “Tech Sisters” circle, will I be expected to be the emotional support for everyone else, or will there be space for me to just be tired and not okay?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: You mentioned your company’s program—what if I accept an internship there, but then realize the culture is still dominated by microaggressions? Is there a way to report it without being labeled a complainer?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: Sometimes I wonder if I’m even supposed to be here. Is that feeling normal, or am I just not cut out for this?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: What if I become successful one day, but I still don’t see myself reflected in the leaders around me? Will I feel lonely at the top?

prompt: When you invited us to lunch today, did you know we’d cry? And if you did—did you plan for that, or did you just show up and hope it would help?

prompt: I just mentioned the 200% increase in female applicants after middle-school coding camps—could that number be misleading if the camps were heavily subsidized and not scalable to rural or underfunded districts, and should I have acknowledged that limitation?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: The interviewer just showed a photo of a classroom where only one girl is raising her hand during a coding lesson—how do I reframe that image as a systemic issue, not a reflection of girls’ aptitude, without making the teacher feel blamed?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: If I say computational thinking should be woven into math class like fractions or geometry, what’s the most convincing example I can give of a 3rd grader using loops to solve a pattern problem—something real, not theoretical?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: I’ve been told that some school districts are removing tech electives to focus on “core subjects”—how do I argue that computational thinking *is* a core subject, especially when state standardized tests don’t measure it?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: The interviewer asked if boys are naturally more drawn to tech toys—what if I push back too hard and unintentionally alienate parents or educators who believe in innate differences, even if they’re well-meaning?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: One of the pilot programs I mentioned is in a district where 80% of students are Latino and 60% are girls—what if someone asks why we’re not also addressing cultural expectations around girls’ roles in those communities, and how do I answer without stereotyping?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: I’m sitting across from the interviewer who just admitted she never took a computer class until college—how do I make her feel included in this conversation, not guilty, so she becomes an ally instead of a critic?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: A teacher emailed me yesterday saying her 4th-grade girls loved the coding blocks but their parents thought it was “just play”—how do I help educators convince skeptical families that this isn’t a hobby, but a literacy?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: What if a parent in the audience later tells me their daughter quit coding after being told “you’re not the type” by a male peer—how do I help them rebuild her confidence without oversimplifying the emotional toll?

prompt: I cited data from our outreach programs, but what if someone asks how we measured “interest” in those girls—was it sign-ups, retention, self-reported confidence, or something else, and does that metric actually predict long-term success?

prompt: The Returnship program is designed for women who left tech for caregiving, but how do we ensure it doesn’t unintentionally reinforce the idea that their absence was a “pause” rather than a systemic failure of our workplace culture to accommodate caregivers?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: One of our returning participants shared that she felt like she was “starting over” after five years away—how do we design mentorship and onboarding so she doesn’t just re-enter the company, but reclaims her voice and authority within it?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: We’ve identified “lack of visible role models” as a top reason for attrition—yet our senior engineering leadership remains 80% male. How do we accelerate representation without tokenizing the few women who are already there?

prompt: We cooked together tonight—she made the pasta from scratch while I chopped vegetables. I’ve never seen her so focused. Do you think hands-on, non-digital creativity like this helps balance the mental load of learning to code?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: She said her AP CS teacher “doesn’t get why anyone would want to code for mental health.” Is that a sign we need to train educators not just in curriculum, but in empathetic tech vision?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: Tonight, she asked if I ever felt like an imposter when I first led a team. I told her yes—but I didn’t tell her how often I still feel it. Should I be more honest about that? Does vulnerability help or hurt in modeling resilience?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: What if her app gets adopted by her school—but then becomes a tool for surveillance instead of support? How do we build ethical guardrails into student-led tech projects before they go live?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: We watched the episode where everyone realizes they’ve been in hell the whole time… and she said, “That’s kind of what it feels like sometimes in my coding group.” Is she hinting at something deeper about belonging in her class?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: I’ve spent my career advocating for women in tech—but tonight, I realized I’ve never asked her what *she* needs from me as a mom, not just as a company leader. What’s the difference between supporting her passion and projecting my own legacy onto it?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: Could measuring success by “parity” in tech companies unintentionally pressure organizations to prioritize numbers over genuine inclusion, and how do I design this vision so it doesn’t become performative?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: If every CS department hits 50% female enrollment by 2030, what happens to the men in those programs—do they feel sidelined, or do we create a culture where equity lifts everyone without erasing their place?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: What if the most passionate young women in computing are the ones who don’t even know this vision exists—how do I make sure the message doesn’t just echo in auditoriums but reaches the quiet, isolated coders in rural towns and under-resourced schools?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: When I write “You belong here,” am I giving permission—or am I, in some small way, taking responsibility for a system that still makes them question it every day?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: How do I respond if a major tech company tells me they can’t measure parity because “we hire based on merit alone”—and how do I help them see that merit is shaped by environment, not just ability?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: What if the keynote speech is received with applause—but then nothing changes in the classrooms, the hiring pipelines, or the funding allocations? How do I keep the momentum alive beyond the stage?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: Is it possible that by setting such an ambitious, quantifiable goal, I’m unintentionally making it harder for departments that are at 20% or 30% to feel like they’re making progress—how do I celebrate incremental wins without diluting the vision?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: Who am I really speaking to when I draft this speech—the administrators, the students, the skeptics, or the version of myself who once sat alone in a lecture hall wondering if she’d ever fit in?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: What if the 50% target is achieved—but the women who enter those departments still leave within five years due to isolation, bias, or burnout? Does parity without retention just mean a revolving door?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: Could this vision unintentionally create a new kind of pressure on young girls to “represent” their gender in every project, every team, every interview—and how do I protect them from that burden?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: If I send this email at sunrise, will it feel like a gift—or like another expectation piled on top of someone already carrying too much?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: What if the tech companies that embrace parity as a metric start to market it as a brand advantage—turning inclusion into a selling point instead of a moral imperative? Do I still celebrate that, even if the motivation is imperfect?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: Who gets left out if we only measure gender parity—what about women of color, LGBTQ+ women, disabled women, or those from low-income backgrounds who face layered barriers even within the 50%?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: When I close my laptop and turn off the light, am I hoping this vision will change the world—or just hoping someone, somewhere, reads it and feels a little less alone tonight?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: Could the quiet moments before sleep be the most powerful time for innovation—when a 14-year-old girl in rural Kansas, tired from homework but still curious, dreams of building an app that helps her grandmother manage diabetes, and no one has ever told her she could?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: If I could send one message back in time to myself as a first-year computer science student—what would it be, and would it have changed the path of every woman I’ve mentored since?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: What if the real barrier isn’t access to code, but access to belief—that deep, unshakable conviction that they belong in the room, even when the room feels like it was built without them?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: How many brilliant young women have walked away from computer science not because they couldn’t do the work, but because they couldn’t find a single person who looked like them and said, “I see you, and you’re not alone”?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: When I think of “diversity in tech,” am I focusing too much on numbers and not enough on the emotional safety it takes for a girl to raise her hand in a male-dominated lecture hall for the first time?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: What does it mean to build a tech future that reflects the diversity of humanity—when the algorithms we train today are shaped by the blind spots of those who coded them yesterday?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: If I could sit with every girl who ever deleted her coding project because she thought it wasn’t “good enough,” what would I say to help her understand that her voice wasn’t the problem—the system was?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: Could the most important code we write not be in Python or Java, but in the stories we tell ourselves—and each other—about who gets to be a creator in this field?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: What if the next breakthrough in AI comes from a teenager who never took a formal CS class, but spent nights tinkering with open-source tools on a library computer—how do I make sure she never feels like an imposter in a world that calls her “lucky” instead of “brilliant”?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: When I close my eyes and imagine the future of tech, am I seeing a world where gender diversity is the norm—or just a goal we’ve been told to chase?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: Who is the girl right now, in a small town or a refugee camp, quietly learning to code on a borrowed phone, unaware that her ideas could change the world—and how do I make sure she never has to wonder if she’s allowed to?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: Is it possible to create equity in tech without first acknowledging that the foundation was built on exclusion—and that true inclusion means redesigning the blueprint, not just adding more chairs to the table?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing

prompt: What if the real measure of success isn’t how many women enter computer science—but how many stay, thrive, and become the ones who open the doors wide enough for others to walk through without looking back?

constraints_data.text: Use an academic style of writing
